questions.text: The Gond kingdom of Garha Katanga had how many villages according to the Akbar Nama?
questions.explanation: The administrative system of these kingdoms was becoming centralised and Garha Katanga was a rich kingdom.

questions.text: Who was the Gond Rani who ruled on behalf of her five-year-old son, Bir Narain?
questions.explanation: Rani Durgawati was a very capable ruler and fought bravely against the Mughal forces led by Asaf Khan.

questions.text: The Ahom state depended upon forced labour. Those forced to work for the state were called:
questions.explanation: A census of the population was taken and each village had to send a number of paiks by rotation.

questions.text: The Ahom society was divided into clans or ________, which often controlled several villages.
questions.explanation: Ahoms built a large state and used firearms as early as the 1530s.

questions.text: Which nomadic pastoralists were important trader-nomads used by Sultan Alauddin Khalji to transport grain to city markets?
questions.explanation: The Banjaras carried grain on their bullocks from different areas and sold it in towns.

questions.text: A caravan of Banjaras was known as a:
questions.explanation: The Banjaras were the most important trader-nomads in medieval India.

questions.text: The Ahom people migrated to the Brahmaputra valley from present-day:
questions.explanation: The Ahoms established their kingdom in the 13th century after suppressing the older bhuiyans.

questions.text: Which tribe was very influential in Punjab during the 13th and 14th centuries?
questions.explanation: Later, the Gakkhars became more important and their chief was made a noble by Emperor Akbar.

questions.text: Tribal societies were generally not divided into numerous ________, unlike caste-based societies.
questions.explanation: Tribal groups were often united by kinship ties and many tribes obtained their livelihood from agriculture.

questions.text: The specialized historical works of the Ahoms were called:
questions.explanation: Buranjis were written first in the Ahom language and then in Assamese.
